title: Practice Set 10 — Platform Migration and Capacity Planning
description: Complete NordGrid’s platform transformation: evaluate Spark upgrade strategies, build 3-year capacity forecasts, design pipeline deprecation policies, produce a version upgrade plan, and deliver a 12-month platform roadmap that sequences all initiatives under resource constraints.

datasets.description: 150-pipeline inventory: Spark versions, status, creation dates, costs, test coverage, documentation. 41 on old Spark. 12 deprecated/inactive. 46 without tests.

tasks.title: Evaluate Migration Strategies for NordGrid’s Spark Upgrade

description.context: NordGrid has 41 pipelines on Spark 3.2/3.3 (end-of-life). The platform team must upgrade all pipelines to Spark 3.5. Three migration strategies are proposed: big-bang (upgrade all at once), phased by team, and phased by Spark version. The migration must not disrupt production billing.
description.task: Evaluate the three strategies against NordGrid’s constraints: 150 pipelines, 11 teams, 46 pipelines without tests, and a billing freeze in Q2 (no billing pipeline changes April–June).
description.validationHint: Big-bang is fastest but riskiest. Phased-by-version is safest (3.2→3.3→3.4→3.5 step-by-step). Phased-by-team lets each team own their migration timeline.

evidence.content:
Option A: Big-Bang
  Upgrade all 150 pipelines to 3.5 in one weekend maintenance window.
  Duration: 1 weekend. Rollback: revert entire cluster.
  Risk: untested pipelines (46) may break silently. Billing disruption if billing pipelines fail.
  Cost: lowest (one migration event).

Option B: Phased by Team
  Each team upgrades their pipelines on their own timeline (6 months total).
  Duration: 6 months. Rollback: per-team revert.
  Risk: version fragmentation during transition (some teams on 3.5, others on 3.3). Cross-team pipelines may have version conflicts.
  Cost: medium (multiple migration events, some teams need support).

Option C: Phased by Spark Version (step-up)
  Upgrade in order: 3.2→3.3 first (19 pipelines), then 3.3→3.4 (22+19=41), then 3.4→3.5 (all 150).
  Duration: 4 months. Each step validated before the next.
  Risk: lowest (each step is a minor version bump with known changelogs). Billing can skip the Q2 freeze window.
  Cost: highest (3 migration rounds).

Constraints:
  - 46 pipelines have no tests (risk of silent breakage)
  - Billing freeze: April–June, no billing pipeline changes
  - 41 pipelines on 3.2/3.3 (end-of-life, security patches ending)

fields.label: Which strategy should be ELIMINATED first?
options: Option A (big-bang) — upgrading 150 pipelines in one weekend with 46 untested pipelines is unacceptable risk. A single silent breakage in billing during the weekend could go undetected until Monday’s reconciliation.
Option B (phased by team) — version fragmentation causes cross-team conflicts
Option C (phased by version) — too slow and expensive
None can be eliminated — all are viable
fields.correctAnswer: Option A (big-bang) — upgrading 150 pipelines in one weekend with 46 untested pipelines is unacceptable risk. A single silent breakage in billing during the weekend could go undetected until Monday’s reconciliation.

fields.label: Which strategy do you recommend?
options: Option B — team autonomy is the highest priority

Option C (phased by version) — each step is a minor version bump with known breaking changes. The platform team can write a compatibility guide per step. 3.2→3.3 affects only 19 pipelines (smallest blast radius). Billing pipelines on 3.4 can defer the 3.4→3.5 step until after the Q2 freeze.
A hybrid: Option C for the upgrade path, but Option B for execution. Platform team defines the version-step plan; each team executes within the plan at their own pace.
Both Option C and the hybrid are defensible. Option C is more controlled. The hybrid gives teams autonomy within a structured path.
fields.correctAnswer: Both Option C and the hybrid are defensible. Option C is more controlled. The hybrid gives teams autonomy within a structured path.
fields.secondaryCorrectAnswer: Option C (phased by version) — each step is a minor version bump with known breaking changes. The platform team can write a compatibility guide per step. 3.2→3.3 affects only 19 pipelines (smallest blast radius). Billing pipelines on 3.4 can defer the 3.4→3.5 step until after the Q2 freeze.

fields.label: What must happen BEFORE any upgrade for the 46 untested pipelines?
options: Nothing — upgrade and fix issues as they arise

Write basic smoke tests (row count, schema validation, null rate check) for each untested pipeline BEFORE upgrading. Without tests, there is no way to detect silent breakage. The test-writing sprint is a prerequisite, not a parallel workstream.
Remove the 46 untested pipelines from the upgrade scope
Run the upgrade in a staging environment only
fields.correctAnswer: Write basic smoke tests (row count, schema validation, null rate check) for each untested pipeline BEFORE upgrading. Without tests, there is no way to detect silent breakage. The test-writing sprint is a prerequisite, not a parallel workstream.

description.context: NordGrid’s current monthly compute cost is $140,929 across 150 pipelines. Growth projections: meters grow 20% year-over-year (3.2M → 3.84M → 4.6M → 5.5M), data volume grows 25% YoY (pipeline data proportional to meter count + new use cases), and 10 new pipelines are added per year. The platform team must forecast when current cluster capacity is exhausted.
description.task: Produce a 3-year capacity forecast as a DataFrame. Each row is one year (2027, 2028, 2029). Columns include projected meters, pipelines, monthly cost, and capacity status (sufficient/at-risk/exceeded).
description.validationHint: Year 1: 150 + 10 = 160 pipelines, cost × 1.25. Year 2: 170 pipelines, cost × 1.25². Year 3: 180 pipelines, cost × 1.25³. Current cluster handles ~$180K/month; forecast when cost exceeds this.

evidence.content:
Current (2026):
  Meters: 3,200,000
  Pipelines: 150
  Monthly compute: $140,929
  Cluster capacity: ~$180,000/month equivalent (before needing new nodes)

Growth projections:
  Meters: +20% YoY
  Data volume / compute: +25% YoY (proportional to meters + new streaming workloads)
  New pipelines: +10 per year (new use cases, ML models, compliance requirements)
  Efficiency gains from optimization: -5% per year (AQE, bucketing, partitioning improvements)

fields.label: When will NordGrid’s current cluster capacity be exhausted?
options: Never — efficiency gains offset growth
Mid-2027 (Year 1) — 25% growth minus 5% efficiency = 20% net growth. $140,929 × 1.20 = $169,115. Approaching $180K capacity. By mid-2027, seasonal peaks will exceed capacity.
2028 (Year 2) — $169,115 × 1.20 = $202,938. Exceeds $180K. Cluster expansion needed by early 2028.
2029 (Year 3) — plenty of headroom
fields.correctAnswer: 2028 (Year 2) — $169,115 × 1.20 = $202,938. Exceeds $180K. Cluster expansion needed by early 2028.
fields.secondaryCorrectAnswer: Mid-2027 (Year 1) — 25% growth minus 5% efficiency = 20% net growth. $140,929 × 1.20 = $169,115. Approaching $180K capacity. By mid-2027, seasonal peaks will exceed capacity.

starterScaffold.content:
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import col, lit, round as spark_round

spark = SparkSession.builder.appName('nordgrid-capacity-forecast').master('local[*]').getOrCreate()

# Current baseline
current_meters = 3200000
current_pipelines = 150
current_monthly_cost = 140929
cluster_capacity_usd = 180000

# Growth rates
meter_growth = 0.20
compute_growth = 0.25
efficiency_gain = 0.05
net_growth = compute_growth - efficiency_gain  # 20%
new_pipelines_per_year = 10

# YOUR SOLUTION BELOW
# Produce a 3-year forecast as DataFrame.
# Each row = one year (2027, 2028, 2029).
# Required columns: year, projected_meters, projected_pipelines,
#   projected_monthly_cost, cluster_capacity_usd, capacity_status,
#   action_needed
# capacity_status: 'sufficient' if cost < 80% capacity,
#   'at_risk' if 80-100%, 'exceeded' if > 100%
# Store in 'forecast'



# YOUR SOLUTION ABOVE

# Verification
print('3-Year Capacity Forecast:')
forecast.show(truncate=False)

assertions.check: all(c in forecast.columns for c in ['year', 'projected_meters', 'projected_monthly_cost', 'capacity_status'])

assertions.description: At least one year shows at_risk or exceeded

description.context: NordGrid has 12 deprecated/inactive pipelines and 10 active pipelines with zero consumers. These 22 pipelines consume minimal resources ($538+/month) but create maintenance burden: they appear in monitoring dashboards, consume CI/CD slots, and confuse new engineers. The platform team wants a deprecation policy.
description.task: Design the deprecation lifecycle: criteria for identifying candidates, grace period, notification process, data retention after shutdown, and exception handling for pipelines that are ‘inactive but critical.’
description.validationHint: The hardest deprecation cases are active pipelines with zero visible consumers — they might have undocumented consumers (scripts, notebooks, manual queries). The policy needs a discovery phase before shutdown.

evidence.content:
Category 1: Deprecated (11 pipelines)
  Status: deprecated, still running on schedule
  Last meaningful output: 2-12 months ago
  Consumer count: 0
  Risk of shutdown: low (already flagged as deprecated)

Category 2: Inactive (1 pipeline)
  Status: inactive, not running
  Last run: 8 months ago
  Consumer count: 0
  Risk: none (already stopped)

Category 3: Active but zero consumers (10 pipelines)
  Status: active, running daily
  Consumer count: 0 in catalog
  Risk: UNKNOWN — may have undocumented consumers (ad-hoc notebooks, direct file reads)
  Monthly cost: ~$8,400

Total maintenance burden: 22 pipelines × 2 hours/month monitoring = 44 hours/month

fields.label: How should Category 3 (active, zero consumers) be handled differently from Category 1 (deprecated)?
options: Shut them down immediately — zero consumers means no impact

Discovery phase first: announce planned shutdown 30 days in advance. Monitor access logs for any reads (scripts, notebooks, direct file access). If undocumented consumers surface, re-classify the pipeline as active with consumers. If no access detected in 30 days, proceed to shutdown.
Keep them running indefinitely — they might be needed someday
Treat them identically to Category 1 — deprecated status
fields.correctAnswer: Discovery phase first: announce planned shutdown 30 days in advance. Monitor access logs for any reads (scripts, notebooks, direct file access). If undocumented consumers surface, re-classify the pipeline as active with consumers. If no access detected in 30 days, proceed to shutdown.

fields.label: How long should output data be retained after a pipeline is shut down?
options: Delete immediately — the pipeline is no longer needed

90 days for non-compliance tables, 7 years for compliance tables. 90 days allows time to discover a mistake and re-derive if needed. Compliance retention is a legal requirement regardless of pipeline status.
Forever — storage is cheap
30 days — minimum viable retention
fields.correctAnswer: 90 days for non-compliance tables, 7 years for compliance tables. 90 days allows time to discover a mistake and re-derive if needed. Compliance retention is a legal requirement regardless of pipeline status.

fields.label: What criteria should automatically flag a pipeline for deprecation review?
options: Any pipeline older than 2 years

No runs in 90 days OR zero consumers for 60 days OR owner team disbanded. These criteria catch the three common deprecation patterns: abandoned (no runs), orphaned (no consumers), and headless (no owner).
Monthly cost below $100
Pipeline has no documentation
fields.correctAnswer: No runs in 90 days OR zero consumers for 60 days OR owner team disbanded. These criteria catch the three common deprecation patterns: abandoned (no runs), orphaned (no consumers), and headless (no owner).

description.context: NordGrid will upgrade from mixed Spark 3.2-3.4 to Spark 3.5. The platform team needs a detailed upgrade plan: compatibility scan, test matrix, phased rollout schedule, rollback triggers, and validation criteria. The plan must account for the Q2 billing freeze (April–June: no billing pipeline changes).
description.task: Produce the upgrade plan as a DataFrame. Each row is one phase with scope, timeline, entry criteria, validation checks, and rollback triggers.
description.validationHint: Minimum phases: pre-scan (compatibility), test-sprint (write missing tests), pilot (1 non-critical pipeline), wave-1 (non-billing), wave-2 (billing, after Q2 freeze), validation, cleanup.

evidence.content:
Pipelines by current version:
  Spark 3.2.1: 19 pipelines (end-of-life, security risk)
  Spark 3.3.0: 22 pipelines
  Spark 3.4.2: 57 pipelines
  Spark 3.5.1: 52 pipelines (already current)

Key constraints:
  - 46 active pipelines have no tests
  - Q2 billing freeze: April–June, no billing pipeline changes
  - 8 critical SLA pipelines must not have downtime > 5 minutes
  - ml-platform has custom UDFs that may break on version change

fields.label: What is the correct order to upgrade Spark versions?
options: Jump all pipelines directly to 3.5
Step-up: 3.2→3.3, then 3.3→3.4, then 3.4→3.5. Each step has a known changelog. Direct 3.2→3.5 jumps skip 2 versions of breaking changes, making failures harder to diagnose.
Upgrade in reverse: start with 3.4→3.5 (largest group) for maximum impact
Random order — version order doesn’t matter
fields.correctAnswer: Step-up: 3.2→3.3, then 3.3→3.4, then 3.4→3.5. Each step has a known changelog. Direct 3.2→3.5 jumps skip 2 versions of breaking changes, making failures harder to diagnose.

starterScaffold.content:
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import col, lit

spark = SparkSession.builder.appName('nordgrid-upgrade-plan').master('local[*]').getOrCreate()

df = spark.read.csv('PX10_data_platform_inventory.csv', header=True, inferSchema=True)

print(f'Total pipelines: {df.count()}')
print(f'Need upgrade: {df.filter(col("spark_version") != "3.5.1").count()}')
print(f'Without tests: {df.filter((col("has_tests") == "no") & (col("status") == "active")).count()}')

# YOUR SOLUTION BELOW
# Produce upgrade plan as DataFrame.
# Each row = one phase.
# Required columns: phase_number, phase_name, scope, timeline_weeks,
#   entry_criteria, validation_checks, rollback_trigger, affected_pipelines
# Minimum 7 phases.
# Account for: test-writing sprint, Q2 billing freeze, critical SLA pipelines.
# Store in 'upgrade_plan'



# YOUR SOLUTION ABOVE

# Verification
print('Spark Upgrade Plan:')
upgrade_plan.show(truncate=False)
print(f'Total phases: {upgrade_plan.count()}')

assertions.check: all(c in upgrade_plan.columns for c in ['phase_number', 'phase_name', 'scope', 'timeline_weeks', 'rollback_trigger'])

description.context: NordGrid’s CTO wants a 12-month platform roadmap that combines the Spark upgrade (Task 1/4), capacity planning (Task 2), pipeline deprecation (Task 3), and new workload onboarding. The roadmap must sequence these initiatives to minimize risk and maximize impact.
description.task: Produce the roadmap as a DataFrame. Each row is one initiative with its quarter, dependencies, teams involved, expected benefit, and risk. The roadmap must account for the Q2 billing freeze and resource constraints (the platform team has 8 engineers).
description.validationHint: The roadmap must sequence correctly: test-writing before upgrade, deprecation before capacity expansion (removing waste before adding capacity), upgrade before new workload onboarding (onboard on the latest version). Q2 billing freeze blocks billing-related work April–June.

initiatives.label: Approved initiatives
initiatives.content:
1. Spark 3.5 upgrade: 41 pipelines need upgrade (Tasks 1, 4)
2. Capacity expansion: cluster expansion needed by early 2028 (Task 2)
3. Pipeline deprecation: 22 candidates for cleanup (Task 3)
4. Test coverage sprint: 46 untested pipelines need smoke tests
5. New workload onboarding: 3 new ML pipelines, 2 new compliance reports
6. Streaming migration: 23 pipelines from batch to streaming (PX5)
7. Schema governance rollout: contract tests for 400 schemas (PX4)

constraints.label: Resource and calendar constraints
constraints.content:
Platform team: 8 engineers (cannot run >2 major initiatives in parallel)
Q2 billing freeze: April–June 2026 (no billing pipeline changes)
Budget: $200K annual compute + $50K for new initiatives
Dependencies: upgrade before onboarding, tests before upgrade, deprecation frees capacity

fields.label: What is the correct initiative sequence for Q1 2026 (January–March)?
options: Start with the Spark upgrade immediately

Q1: Test coverage sprint (46 untested pipelines) + pipeline deprecation (22 candidates). Tests are a prerequisite for the upgrade. Deprecation frees capacity and reduces upgrade scope. Both can run in parallel with 4 engineers each.
Q1: Streaming migration — highest business value
Q1: Schema governance — prevents future problems
fields.correctAnswer: Q1: Test coverage sprint (46 untested pipelines) + pipeline deprecation (22 candidates). Tests are a prerequisite for the upgrade. Deprecation frees capacity and reduces upgrade scope. Both can run in parallel with 4 engineers each.

fields.label: How should the Q2 billing freeze affect the roadmap?
options: Ignore the freeze — upgrades are infrastructure, not billing changes

Q2: upgrade non-billing pipelines (3.2→3.3→3.4 step-up). Defer billing pipeline upgrade to Q3. Use Q2 to complete the upgrade for the 100+ non-billing pipelines while billing is frozen. This is the most efficient use of the freeze window.
Pause all platform work during Q2
Move the billing freeze to Q3 to allow upgrades
fields.correctAnswer: Q2: upgrade non-billing pipelines (3.2→3.3→3.4 step-up). Defer billing pipeline upgrade to Q3. Use Q2 to complete the upgrade for the 100+ non-billing pipelines while billing is frozen. This is the most efficient use of the freeze window.

fields.label: When should new workload onboarding begin?
options: Immediately — new workloads can’t wait for the upgrade

After the Spark 3.5 upgrade completes (Q3-Q4). Onboarding new pipelines on 3.5 from the start avoids re-upgrading them later. The 3 ML and 2 compliance pipelines are not urgent enough to justify onboarding on 3.4 and re-upgrading in Q3.
In parallel with the upgrade — different teams
Never — focus on existing pipelines
fields.correctAnswer: After the Spark 3.5 upgrade completes (Q3-Q4). Onboarding new pipelines on 3.5 from the start avoids re-upgrading them later. The 3 ML and 2 compliance pipelines are not urgent enough to justify onboarding on 3.4 and re-upgrading in Q3.

starterScaffold.content:
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import col, lit

spark = SparkSession.builder.appName('nordgrid-roadmap').master('local[*]').getOrCreate()

df = spark.read.csv('PX10_data_platform_inventory.csv', header=True, inferSchema=True)

# YOUR SOLUTION BELOW
# Produce 12-month platform roadmap as DataFrame.
# Each row = one initiative in a specific quarter.
# Required columns: initiative_id, initiative_name, quarter,
#   description, dependencies, teams_involved, engineers_needed,
#   expected_benefit, risk_level
# Minimum 8 initiatives across 4 quarters.
# Max 2 major initiatives per quarter (8 engineers constraint).
# Store in 'roadmap'



# YOUR SOLUTION ABOVE

# Verification
print('12-Month Platform Roadmap:')
roadmap.show(truncate=False)
print(f'Total initiatives: {roadmap.count()}')
for q in ['Q1', 'Q2', 'Q3', 'Q4']:
    count = roadmap.filter(col('quarter') == q).count()
    print(f'  {q}: {count} initiatives')

assertions.check: all(c in roadmap.columns for c in ['initiative_name', 'quarter', 'dependencies', 'expected_benefit'])

assertions.description: Test sprint appears before or in same quarter as upgrade
No more than ~4 initiatives per quarter (resource constraint)

conceptsCombined: Capacity forecasting and growth modeling (Task 2, Lessons 4-5)
Upgrade execution plan with test prerequisites (Task 4, Lessons 8-9)
Initiative sequencing under resource and calendar constraints (Lesson 10)
Cross-initiative dependencies from PX4, PX5, PX8, PX9
synthesisNotes.rationale: This is the capstone of the entire senior track. The student sequences 7 platform initiatives across 4 quarters under 3 constraints (8 engineers, Q2 billing freeze, initiative dependencies). Every decision from the senior track contributes: PX1’s cluster architecture, PX4’s governance, PX5’s streaming migration, PX8’s resource pools, PX9’s DR. The roadmap is the artifact a VP of Engineering would present to the CTO.

selfCheck.scaleMatters: 150 pipelines, 11 teams, 3-year forecast, 12-month roadmap. Single-pipeline upgrade is mid-level.
selfCheck.theoryPracticeBoundary: Theory teaches migration and planning concepts. Practice requires sequencing 7 initiatives across 4 quarters under real constraints (billing freeze, team capacity, dependencies).
